In [rail transport](/wiki/Rail_transport "Rail transport"), **track gauge** is the distance between the two rails of a [railway track](/wiki/Railway_track "Railway track"). All vehicles on a rail network must have [wheelsets](/wiki/Wheelset_(rail_transport) "Wheelset (rail transport)") that are compatible with the track gauge. Since many different track gauges exist worldwide, gauge differences often present a barrier to wider operation on railway networks.

The term derives from the metal bar, or gauge, that is used to ensure the distance between the rails is correct.

Railways also deploy two other gauges to ensure compliance with a required standard. A *[loading gauge](/wiki/Loading_gauge "Loading gauge")* is a two-dimensional profile that encompasses a cross-section of the track, a rail vehicle and a maximum-sized load: all rail vehicles and their loads must be contained in the corresponding envelope. A *[structure gauge](/wiki/Structure_gauge "Structure gauge")* specifies the outline into which structures (bridges, platforms, lineside equipment etc.) must not encroach.

## Uses of the term

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Track_gauge&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Uses of the term")]

The most common use of the term "track gauge" refers to the transverse distance between the inside surfaces of the two load-bearing rails of a [railway track](/wiki/Railway_track "Railway track"), usually measured at 12.7 millimetres (0.50 inches) to 15.9 millimetres (0.63 inches) below the top of the rail head in order to clear worn corners and allow for rail heads having sloping sides.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The term derives from the "gauge", a metal bar with a precisely positioned lug at each end that track crews use to ensure the actual distance between the rails lies within tolerances of a prescribed standard: on curves, for example, the spacing is wider than normal.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Deriving from the name of the bar, the distance between these rails is also referred to as the track gauge.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

### Early track gauges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Track_gauge&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early track gauges")]

See also: [Permanent way (history)](/wiki/History_of_the_railway_track "History of the railway track"), [Wagonway](/wiki/Wagonway "Wagonway"), and [Plateway](/wiki/Plateway "Plateway")

The earliest form of railway was a wooden wagonway, along which single wagons were manhandled, almost always in or from a mine or quarry. Initially the wagons were guided by human muscle power; subsequently by various mechanical methods. Timber rails wore rapidly: later, flat cast-iron plates were provided to limit the wear. In some localities, the plates were made L-shaped, with the vertical part of the L guiding the wheels; this is generally referred to as a "plateway". Flanged wheels eventually became universal, and the spacing between the rails had to be compatible with that of the wagon wheels.[[4]](#cite_note-lewis-4)

As the guidance of the wagons was improved, short strings of wagons could be connected and pulled by teams of horses, and the track could be extended from the immediate vicinity of the mine or quarry, typically to a navigable waterway. The wagons were built to a consistent pattern and the track would be made to suit the needs of the horses and wagons: the gauge was more critical. The [Penydarren Tramroad](/wiki/Merthyr_Tramroad "Merthyr Tramroad") of 1802 in South Wales, a plateway, spaced these at 4 ft 4 in (1,321 mm) over the outside of the upstands.[[5]](#cite_note-cragg-5)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Chpr_rail.jpg/250px-Chpr_rail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chpr_rail.jpg)

Fish-belly cast-iron rails from the Cromford and High Peak Railway

The Penydarren Tramroad probably carried the first journey by a locomotive, in 1804, and it was successful for the locomotive, but unsuccessful for the track: the plates were not strong enough to carry its weight. A considerable progressive step was made when cast iron edge rails were first employed; these had the major axis of the rail section configured vertically, giving a much stronger section to resist bending forces, and this was further improved when fish-belly rails were introduced.[[6]](#cite_note-earlyrailways-6)

Edge rails required a close match between rail spacing and the configuration of the wheelsets, and the importance of the gauge was reinforced. Railways were still seen as local concerns: there was no appreciation of a future connection to other lines, and the choice of track gauge was still a pragmatic decision based on local requirements and prejudices, and probably determined by existing local designs of (road) vehicles.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Thus, the [Monkland and Kirkintilloch Railway](/wiki/Monkland_and_Kirkintilloch_Railway "Monkland and Kirkintilloch Railway") (1826) in the West of Scotland used 4 ft 6 in (1,372 mm);[[7]](#cite_note-martin-7) the [Dundee and Newtyle Railway](/wiki/Dundee_and_Newtyle_Railway "Dundee and Newtyle Railway") (1831) in the north-east of Scotland adopted 4 ft 6+1⁄2 in (1,384 mm);[[8]](#cite_note-ferguson-8) the [Redruth and Chasewater Railway](/wiki/Redruth_and_Chasewater_Railway "Redruth and Chasewater Railway") (1825) in Cornwall chose 4 ft (1,219 mm).[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The [Arbroath and Forfar Railway](/wiki/Arbroath_and_Forfar_Railway "Arbroath and Forfar Railway") opened in 1838 with a gauge of 5 ft 6 in (1,676 mm),[[10]](#cite_note-whishaw-10) and the [Ulster Railway](/wiki/Ulster_Railway "Ulster Railway") of 1839 used 6 ft 2 in (1,880 mm).[[10]](#cite_note-whishaw-10)

### "Standard" gauge appears

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Track_gauge&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: \"Standard\" gauge appears")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Killingworth-locomotive.jpg/250px-Killingworth-locomotive.jpg)](/wiki/File:Killingworth-locomotive.jpg)

An early Stephenson locomotive

Locomotives were being developed in the first decades of the 19th century; they took various forms, but [George Stephenson](/wiki/George_Stephenson "George Stephenson") developed a successful locomotive on the [Killingworth Wagonway](/wiki/Killingworth_Wagonway "Killingworth Wagonway"), where he worked. His designs were successful, and when the [Stockton and Darlington Railway](/wiki/Stockton_and_Darlington_Railway "Stockton and Darlington Railway") was opened in 1825, it used his locomotives, with the same gauge as the [Killingworth line](/wiki/Killingworth_line "Killingworth line"), 4 ft 8 in (1,422 mm).[[11]](#cite_note-tomlinson-11)[[12]](#cite_note-wood-12)

The Stockton and Darlington line was very successful, and when the [Liverpool and Manchester Railway](/wiki/Liverpool_and_Manchester_Railway "Liverpool and Manchester Railway"), the first intercity line, was opened in 1830, it used the same gauge. It too was very successful, and the gauge, widened to 4 ft 8+1⁄2 in or 1,435 mm[[11]](#cite_note-tomlinson-11) and named "[standard gauge](/wiki/Standard_gauge "Standard gauge")", was well on its way to becoming the established norm.

### Gauge differences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Track_gauge&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Gauge differences")]

The Liverpool and Manchester was quickly followed by other trunk railways, with the [Grand Junction Railway](/wiki/Grand_Junction_Railway "Grand Junction Railway") and the [London and Birmingham Railway](/wiki/London_and_Birmingham_Railway "London and Birmingham Railway") forming a huge preponderance of [standard gauge](/wiki/Standard_gauge "Standard gauge"). When Bristol promoters planned a line from London, they employed the innovative engineer [Isambard Kingdom Brunel](/wiki/Isambard_Kingdom_Brunel "Isambard Kingdom Brunel"). He decided on a wider gauge, to give greater stability, and the [Great Western Railway](/wiki/Great_Western_Railway "Great Western Railway") adopted a gauge of 7 ft (2,134 mm), later eased to [7 ft 1⁄4 in](/wiki/7-foot-%C2%BC-inch_gauge_railway "7-foot-¼-inch gauge railway") (2,140 mm). This became known as *[broad gauge](/wiki/Broad_gauge "Broad gauge")*. The [Great Western Railway](/wiki/Great_Western_Railway "Great Western Railway") (GWR) was successful and was greatly expanded, directly and through friendly associated companies, widening the scope of broad gauge.

At the same time, other parts of Britain built railways to standard gauge, and British technology was exported to European countries and parts of North America, also using standard gauge. Britain polarised into two areas: those that used [broad gauge](/wiki/Brunel_gauge "Brunel gauge") and those that used standard gauge. In this context, standard gauge was referred to as "narrow gauge" to indicate the contrast. Some smaller concerns selected other non-standard gauges: the [Eastern Counties Railway](/wiki/Eastern_Counties_Railway "Eastern Counties Railway") adopted [5 ft](/wiki/5_ft_and_1520_mm_gauge_railways "5 ft and 1520 mm gauge railways") (1,524 mm). Most of them converted to standard gauge at an early date, but the GWR's broad gauge continued to grow.

The larger railway companies wished to expand geographically, and large areas were considered to be under their control. When a new independent line was proposed to open up an unconnected area, the gauge was crucial in determining the allegiance that the line would adopt: if it was broad gauge, it must be friendly to the Great Western railway; if narrow (standard) gauge, it must favour the other companies. The battle to persuade or coerce that choice became very intense, and became referred to as the ["gauge wars"](/wiki/British_Gauge_War "British Gauge War").

As passenger and freight transport between the two areas became increasingly important, the difficulty of moving from one gauge to the other—the *[break-of-gauge](/wiki/Break-of-gauge "Break-of-gauge")*—became more prominent and more objectionable. In 1845 a [Royal Commission on Railway Gauges](/wiki/Royal_Commission_on_Railway_Gauges "Royal Commission on Railway Gauges") was created to look into the growing problem, and this led to the [Regulating the Gauge of Railways Act 1846](/wiki/Regulating_the_Gauge_of_Railways_Act_1846 "Regulating the Gauge of Railways Act 1846"),[[13]](#cite_note-13) which forbade the construction of broad gauge lines unconnected with the broad gauge network. The broad gauge network was eventually converted—a progressive process completed in 1892, called [gauge conversion](/wiki/Track_gauge_conversion "Track gauge conversion"). The same Act mandated the gauge of [5 ft 3 in](/wiki/5_ft_3_in_gauge_railways "5 ft 3 in gauge railways") (1,600 mm) for use in Ireland.

[Triple gauge](/wiki/Triple_gauge "Triple gauge"), from left: 1,435 mm (4 ft 8+1⁄2 in), 1,000 mm (3 ft 3+3⁄8 in), and 600 mm (1 ft 11+5⁄8 in), on display at the [China Railway Museum](/wiki/China_Railway_Museum "China Railway Museum") in [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing")

As railways were built in other countries, the gauge selection was pragmatic: the track would have to fit the rolling stock. If locomotives were imported from elsewhere, especially in the early days, the track would be built to fit them. In some cases standard gauge was adopted, but many countries or companies chose a different gauge as their national gauge, either by governmental policy, or as a matter of individual choice.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## Terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Track_gauge&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Terminology")]

*Standard gauge* is generally known world-wide as being 1,435 mm (4 ft 8+1⁄2 in). Terms such as *broad gauge* and *narrow gauge* do not have any fixed meaning beyond being materially wider or narrower than standard.

In British practice, the space between the rails of a track is colloquially referred to as the "four-foot", and the space between two tracks the "six-foot", descriptions relating to the respective dimensions.

### Standard gauge

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Track_gauge&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Standard gauge")]

Main article: [Standard-gauge railway](/wiki/Standard-gauge_railway "Standard-gauge railway")

In modern usage the term "standard gauge" refers to 1,435 mm (4 ft 8+1⁄2 in). Standard gauge is dominant in a majority of countries, including those in North America, most of western Europe, North Africa, the Middle East, and China.

### Broad gauge

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Track_gauge&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Broad gauge")]

Main article: [Broad-gauge railway](/wiki/Broad-gauge_railway "Broad-gauge railway")

In modern usage, the term "broad gauge" generally refers to track spaced significantly wider than 1,435 mm (4 ft 8+1⁄2 in).

Broad gauge is the dominant gauge in countries in the Indian subcontinent, the former Soviet Union ([CIS](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Independent_States "Commonwealth of Independent States") states, Baltic states, Georgia and Ukraine), Mongolia, Finland (which still uses the original Russian imperial gauge of 1524mm), Spain, Portugal, Argentina, Chile and Ireland. It is also used for the suburban railway systems in [South Australia](/wiki/Railways_in_Adelaide "Railways in Adelaide"), and [Victoria](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Victoria "Rail transport in Victoria"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia").

### Medium gauge

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Track_gauge&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Medium gauge")]

The term "medium gauge" had different meanings throughout history, depending on the local dominant gauge in use.

In 1840s, the [1,600 mm](/wiki/5_ft_3_in_gauge_railways "5 ft 3 in gauge railways") (5 ft 3 in) [Irish gauge](/wiki/Irish_gauge "Irish gauge") was considered a medium gauge compared to Brunel's [7 ft 1⁄4 in](/wiki/7-foot-%C2%BC-inch_gauge_railway "7-foot-¼-inch gauge railway") (2,140 mm) [broad gauge](/wiki/Broad_gauge "Broad gauge") and the 1,435 mm (4 ft 8+1⁄2 in) narrow gauge, which became the modern [standard gauge](/wiki/Standard_gauge "Standard gauge").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

### Narrow gauge

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Track_gauge&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Narrow gauge")]

Main article: [Narrow-gauge railway](/wiki/Narrow-gauge_railway "Narrow-gauge railway")

In modern usage, the term "narrow gauge" generally refers to track spaced significantly narrower than 1,435 mm (4 ft 8+1⁄2 in).

Narrow gauge is the dominant or second dominant gauge in countries of Southern, Central, and East Africa; Southeast Asia; Japan; Taiwan; the Philippines; and Central and South America.

During the period known as the "[battle of the gauges](/wiki/Battle_of_the_gauges "Battle of the gauges")", Stephenson's standard gauge was commonly known as "narrow gauge", while Brunel's railway's [7 ft 1⁄4 in](/wiki/7-foot-%C2%BC-inch_gauge_railway "7-foot-¼-inch gauge railway") (2,140 mm) gauge was termed "[broad gauge](/wiki/Broad_gauge "Broad gauge")". Many narrow gauge railways were built in mountainous regions such as [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales"), the [Rocky Mountains](/wiki/Rocky_Mountains "Rocky Mountains") of North America, Central Europe and South America. [Industrial railways](/wiki/Industrial_railway "Industrial railway") and [mine railways](/wiki/Mine_railway "Mine railway") across the world are often narrow gauge. Sugar cane and banana plantations are mostly served by narrow gauges.

### Minimum gauge

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Track_gauge&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Minimum gauge")]

Main article: [Minimum-gauge railway](/wiki/Minimum-gauge_railway "Minimum-gauge railway")

[Very narrow gauges](/wiki/Minimum-gauge_railway "Minimum-gauge railway") of under 2 feet (610 mm) were used for some [industrial railways](/wiki/Industrial_railway "Industrial railway") in space-restricted environments such as [mines](/wiki/Mining "Mining") or farms. The French company [Decauville](/wiki/Decauville "Decauville") developed 500 mm (19+3⁄4 in) and 400 mm (15+3⁄4 in) tracks, mainly for mines; [Heywood](/wiki/Arthur_Percival_Heywood "Arthur Percival Heywood") developed 15 in (381 mm) gauge for [estate railways](/wiki/British_narrow-gauge_railways#Estate_railways "British narrow-gauge railways"). The most common minimum gauges[*[where?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Naming_conventions_(geographic_names) "Wikipedia:Naming conventions (geographic names)")*] were 15 in (381 mm),[[16]](#cite_note-Heywood,_Minimum_Gauge_Railways-16) 400 mm (15+3⁄4 in), 16 in (406 mm), 18 in (457 mm), 500 mm (19+3⁄4 in) or 20 in (508 mm).

### Break of gauge

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Track_gauge&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Break of gauge")]

Main articles: [Break of gauge](/wiki/Break_of_gauge "Break of gauge") and [Variable gauge](/wiki/Variable_gauge "Variable gauge")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Break_of_gauge_GWR_Gloucester.jpg/250px-Break_of_gauge_GWR_Gloucester.jpg)](/wiki/File:Break_of_gauge_GWR_Gloucester.jpg)

A cartoon depicting the horrors of goods transfer at the break of gauge at [Gloucester](/wiki/Gloucester_railway_station "Gloucester railway station") in 1843

Through operation between railway networks with different gauges was originally impossible; goods had to be transshipped and passengers had to change trains. This was obviously a major obstacle to convenient transport, and in Great Britain, led to political intervention.

On narrow gauge lines, [rollbocks](/wiki/Rollbock "Rollbock") or [transporter wagons](/wiki/Transporter_wagon "Transporter wagon") are used: standard gauge wagons are carried on narrow gauge lines on these special vehicles, generally with rails of the wider gauge to enable those vehicles to roll on and off at transfer points.

On the [Transmongolian Railway](/wiki/Transmongolian_Railway "Transmongolian Railway"), Russia and Mongolia use [1,520 mm](/wiki/5_ft_and_1520_mm_gauge_railways "5 ft and 1520 mm gauge railways") (4 ft 11+27⁄32 in) while China uses the standard gauge of 1,435 mm. At the border, each carriage is lifted and its [bogies are changed](/wiki/Bogie_exchange "Bogie exchange"). The operation can take several hours for a whole train of many carriages.

Other examples include crossings into or out of the former Soviet Union: Ukraine/Slovakia border on the [Bratislava](/wiki/Bratislava "Bratislava")–[Lviv](/wiki/Lviv "Lviv") train, and the Romania/Moldova border on the [Chișinău](/wiki/Chi%C8%99in%C4%83u "Chișinău")–[Bucharest](/wiki/Bucharest "Bucharest") train.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

A system developed by [Talgo](/wiki/Talgo "Talgo") and [Construcciones y Auxiliar de Ferrocarriles](/wiki/Construcciones_y_Auxiliar_de_Ferrocarriles "Construcciones y Auxiliar de Ferrocarriles") (CAF) of Spain uses variable gauge [wheelsets](/wiki/Wheelset_(rail_transport) "Wheelset (rail transport)"); at the border between France and Spain, [through passenger trains](/wiki/Through_service "Through service") are drawn slowly through an apparatus that alters the gauge of the wheels, which slide laterally on the axles.[[18]](#cite_note-alvarez-18)

A similar system is used between China and Central Asia, and between Poland and Ukraine, using the [SUW 2000](/wiki/SUW_2000 "SUW 2000") and [INTERGAUGE](/wiki/INTERGAUGE "INTERGAUGE") variable axle systems.[[19]](#cite_note-19) China and Poland use standard gauge, while Central Asia and Ukraine use 1,520 mm (4 ft 11+27⁄32 in).

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Track_gauge&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Dual gauge")]

Main article: [Dual gauge](/wiki/Dual_gauge "Dual gauge")

When individual railway companies have chosen different gauges and have needed to share a route where space on the ground is limited, [mixed gauge](/wiki/Dual_gauge "Dual gauge") (or dual gauge) track, in which three (sometimes four) rails are supported in the same track structure, can be necessary. The most frequent need for such track was at the approaches to city terminals or at [break-of-gauge](/wiki/Break_of_gauge "Break of gauge") stations.

* [![Cross-section of French 4-rail dual-gauge track (standard and metre gauges)](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Cross-section_of_4-rail_dual-gauge_track_%28standard_and_metre_gauge%29.png/250px-Cross-section_of_4-rail_dual-gauge_track_%28standard_and_metre_gauge%29.png)](/wiki/File:Cross-section_of_4-rail_dual-gauge_track_(standard_and_metre_gauge).png "Cross-section of French 4-rail dual-gauge track (standard and metre gauges)")

  Cross-section of French 4-rail dual-gauge track (standard and metre gauges)
* [![Cross-section of Australian dual-gauge track – 1600 mm (5 ft 3 in) and 1435 mm (4 ft +1⁄2) gauges](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Cross-section_of_Australian_dual-gauge_railway_track.tif/lossless-page1-250px-Cross-section_of_Australian_dual-gauge_railway_track.tif.png)](/wiki/File:Cross-section_of_Australian_dual-gauge_railway_track.tif "Cross-section of Australian dual-gauge track – 1600 mm (5 ft 3 in) and 1435 mm (4 ft 8+1⁄2 in) gauges")

  Cross-section of Australian dual-gauge track – 1600 mm (5 ft 3 in) and 1435 mm (4 ft 8+1⁄2 in) gauges
* [![Cross-section of triple-gauge track at Gladstone and Peterborough, South Australia, before gauge standardisation. The three gauges require the respective gaps between the outer and inner rails to be different, unlike four-rail dual gauge.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Cross-section_of_Australian_triple-gauge_track.png/250px-Cross-section_of_Australian_triple-gauge_track.png)](/wiki/File:Cross-section_of_Australian_triple-gauge_track.png "Cross-section of triple-gauge track at Gladstone and Peterborough, South Australia, before gauge standardisation. The three gauges require the respective gaps between the outer and inner rails to be different, unlike four-rail dual gauge.")

  Cross-section of triple-gauge track at [Gladstone](/wiki/Gladstone_railway_station,_South_Australia "Gladstone railway station, South Australia") and [Peterborough](/wiki/Peterborough_railway_station,_South_Australia "Peterborough railway station, South Australia"), [South Australia](/wiki/South_Australia "South Australia"), before [gauge standardisation](/wiki/Sydney%E2%80%93Perth_rail_corridor "Sydney–Perth rail corridor"). The three gauges require the respective gaps between the outer and inner rails to be different, unlike four-rail dual gauge.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Mixed_gauge_Sassari.jpg/250px-Mixed_gauge_Sassari.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mixed_gauge_Sassari.jpg)

Mixed gauge track at Sassari, Sardinia: 1,435 mm (4 ft 8+1⁄2 in) [standard gauge](/wiki/Standard_gauge "Standard gauge") and [950 mm](/wiki/Track_gauge_in_Italy "Track gauge in Italy") (3 ft 1+3⁄8 in)

Tracks of multiple gauges involve considerable costs in construction (including signalling work) and complexities in track maintenance, and may require some speed restrictions. They are therefore built only when absolutely necessary. If the difference between the two gauges is large enough – for example between 1,435 mm (4 ft 8+1⁄2 in) [standard gauge](/wiki/Standard_gauge "Standard gauge") and [3 ft 6 in](/wiki/3_ft_6_in_gauge_railways "3 ft 6 in gauge railways") (1,067 mm) – three-rail dual-gauge is possible, but if not – for example between 3 ft 6 in (1,067 mm) and 1,000 mm (3 ft 3+3⁄8 in) [metre gauge](/wiki/Metre_gauge "Metre gauge") – four rails must be used. Dual-gauge rail lines occur (or have occurred) in Argentina, Australia, Brazil, Japan, North Korea, Spain, Switzerland, Tunisia and Vietnam.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Mixed_gauge_track_Didcot.JPG/250px-Mixed_gauge_track_Didcot.JPG)](/wiki/File:Mixed_gauge_track_Didcot.JPG)

Reconstructed [mixed-gauge](/wiki/Dual_gauge "Dual gauge"), 1,435 mm (4 ft 8+1⁄2 in) [standard gauge](/wiki/Standard_gauge "Standard gauge") and [7 ft 1⁄4 in](/wiki/7-foot-%C2%BC-inch_gauge_railway "7-foot-¼-inch gauge railway") (2,140 mm) track at [Didcot Railway Museum](/wiki/Didcot_Railway_Museum "Didcot Railway Museum"), England

On the GWR, there was an extended period between political intervention in 1846 that prevented major expansion of its [7 ft 1⁄4 in](/wiki/7-foot-%C2%BC-inch_gauge_railway "7-foot-¼-inch gauge railway") (2,140 mm) [broad gauge](/wiki/Broad_gauge "Broad gauge")[[note 1]](#cite_note-20) and the final [gauge conversion](/wiki/Track_gauge_conversion "Track gauge conversion") to standard gauge in 1892. During this period, many locations practicality required mixed gauge operation, and in station areas the track configuration was extremely complex. This was compounded by the common rail having to be at the platform side in stations; therefore, in many cases, standard-gauge trains needed to be switched from one side of the track to the other at the approach. A special fixed point arrangement was devised for the purpose, where the track layout was simple enough.[[note 2]](#cite_note-21)

In some cases, mixed gauge trains were operated with wagons of both gauges. For example, MacDermot[[20]](#cite_note-macdermot2-22) wrote:

> In November 1871 a novelty in the shape of a *mixed-gauge* goods train was introduced between Truro and Penzance. It was worked by a narrow-gauge engine, and behind the narrow-gauge trucks came a broad-gauge match-truck with wide buffers and sliding shackles, followed by the broad-gauge trucks. Such trains continued to run in West Cornwall until the abolition of the Broad Gauge; they had to stop or come down to walking pace at all stations where fixed points existed and the narrow portion side-stepped to right or left.

### Triple gauge

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Track_gauge&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Triple gauge")]

Main article: [Dual gauge § Triple gauge](/wiki/Dual_gauge#Triple_gauge "Dual gauge")

In rare situations, three different gauges may converge on to a rail yard and triple-gauge track is needed to meet the operational needs of the break-of-gauge station – most commonly where there is insufficient space to do otherwise. Construction and operation of triple-gauge track and its signalling, however, involves immense cost and disruption, and is undertaken when no other alternative is available.[[21]](#cite_note-23)

## Nominal track gauge

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Track_gauge&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Nominal track gauge")]

The nominal track gauge is the distance between the inner faces of the rails. In current practice, it is specified at a certain distance below the rail head as the inner faces of the rail head (the *gauge faces*) are not necessarily vertical. Some amount of tolerance is necessarily allowed from the nominal gauge to allow for wear, etc.; this tolerance is typically greater for track limited to slower speeds, and tighter for track where higher speeds are expected (as an example, in the US the gauge is allowed to vary between 4 ft 8 in (1,420 mm) to 4 ft 10 in (1,470 mm) for track limited to 10 mph (16 km/h), while 70 mph (110 km/h) track is allowed only 4 ft 8 in (1,420 mm) to 4 ft 9+1⁄2 in (1,460 mm). Given the allowed tolerance, it is a common practice to widen the gauge slightly in curves, particularly those of shorter radius (which are inherently slower speed curves).

Rolling stock on the network must have running gear ([wheelsets](/wiki/Wheelset_(rail_transport) "Wheelset (rail transport)")) that are compatible with the gauge, and therefore the gauge is a key parameter in determining interoperability, but there are many others – see below. In some cases in the earliest days of railways, the railway company saw itself as an infrastructure provider only, and independent hauliers provided wagons suited to the gauge. Colloquially the wagons might be referred to as "four-foot gauge wagons", say, if the track had a gauge of four feet. This nominal value does not equate to the flange spacing, as some freedom is allowed for.

An infrastructure manager might specify new or replacement track components at a slight variation from the nominal gauge for pragmatic reasons.

## Units

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Track_gauge&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Units")]

The gauge is defined in [imperial units](/wiki/Imperial_units "Imperial units"), [metric units](/wiki/Metric_unit "Metric unit") or [SI](/wiki/International_System_of_Units "International System of Units") units.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Imperial units were established in the United Kingdom by the [Weights and Measures Act 1824](/wiki/Weights_and_Measures_Act_1824 "Weights and Measures Act 1824"). The [United States customary units](/wiki/United_States_customary_units "United States customary units") for length did not agree with the imperial system until 1959, when one [international yard](/wiki/International_yard "International yard") was defined as 0.9144 meters and, as derived units, 1 foot (=1⁄3 yd) as 0.3048 meter and 1 inch (=1⁄36 yd) as 25.4 mm.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Temporary way – permanent way

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Track_gauge&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Temporary way – permanent way")]

Main article: [Permanent way](/wiki/Permanent_way "Permanent way")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/East_Side_Access_GCT_cavern_with_work_train.jpg/250px-East_Side_Access_GCT_cavern_with_work_train.jpg)](/wiki/File:East_Side_Access_GCT_cavern_with_work_train.jpg)

Narrow gauge work train in the [East Side Access](/wiki/East_Side_Access "East Side Access") cavern where the Manhattan terminus of the standard gauge [Long Island Rail Road](/wiki/Long_Island_Rail_Road "Long Island Rail Road") was built.

A **temporary way** is the temporary track often used for construction, to be replaced by the [permanent way](/wiki/Permanent_way "Permanent way") (the structure consisting of the rails, fasteners, [sleepers/ties](/wiki/Railroad_tie "Railroad tie") and [ballast](/wiki/Track_ballast "Track ballast") (or slab track), plus the underlying subgrade) when construction nears completion. In many cases narrow-gauge track is used for a temporary way because of the convenience in laying it and changing its location over unimproved ground.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In restricted spaces such as tunnels, the temporary way might be double track even though the tunnel will ultimately be single track. The [Airport Rail Link](/wiki/Airport_Link_Company "Airport Link Company") in Sydney had construction trains of [900 mm](/wiki/900_mm_gauge_railways "900 mm gauge railways") (2 ft 11+7⁄16 in) gauge, which were replaced by permanent tracks of 1,435 mm (4 ft 8+1⁄2 in) gauge.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

During World War I, trench warfare led to a relatively static disposition of infantry, requiring considerable logistics to bring them support staff and supplies (food, ammunition, earthworks materials, etc.). Dense light railway networks using temporary narrow gauge track sections were established by both sides for this purpose.[[22]](#cite_note-wolmar-24)

In 1939 it was proposed to construct the western section of the [Yunnan–Burma Railway](/wiki/Yunnan%E2%80%93Burma_Railway "Yunnan–Burma Railway") using a gauge of 15+1⁄4 in (387 mm), since such tiny or "toy" gauge facilitates the [tightest of curves](/wiki/Minimum_railway_curve_radius "Minimum railway curve radius") in difficult terrain.[[23]](#cite_note-25)

## Maintenance standards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Track_gauge&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Maintenance standards")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Checking_the_gauge_at_Plymouth.JPG/250px-Checking_the_gauge_at_Plymouth.JPG)](/wiki/File:Checking_the_gauge_at_Plymouth.JPG)

Track maintenance workers checking the gauge at [Plymouth](/wiki/Plymouth_railway_station "Plymouth railway station"), England

Infrastructure owners specify permitted variances from the nominal gauge, and the required interventions when non-compliant gauge is detected. For example, the [Federal Railroad Administration](/wiki/Federal_Railroad_Administration "Federal Railroad Administration") in the US specifies that the actual gauge of a 1,435 mm track that is rated for a maximum of 60 mph (96.6 km/h) must be between 4 ft 8 in (1,422 mm) and 4 ft 9.5 in (1,460 mm).[[24]](#cite_note-26)

## Advantages and disadvantages of different track gauges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Track_gauge&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Advantages and disadvantages of different track gauges")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |

Speed, capacity, and economy are generally objectives of rail transport, but there is often an inverse relationship between these priorities. There is a common misconception that a narrower gauge permits a tighter turning radius, but for practical purposes, there is no meaningful relationship between gauge and curvature.[[25]](#cite_note-27)[[26]](#cite_note-28)

### Construction cost

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Track_gauge&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Construction cost")]

Narrower gauge railways usually cost less to build because they are usually lighter in construction, using smaller [cars](/wiki/Railway_car "Railway car") and [locomotives](/wiki/Locomotive "Locomotive") (smaller [loading gauge](/wiki/Loading_gauge "Loading gauge")), as well as smaller [bridges](/wiki/Bridge "Bridge"), smaller [tunnels](/wiki/Tunnel "Tunnel") (smaller [structure gauge](/wiki/Structure_gauge "Structure gauge")).[[27]](#cite_note-29) Narrow gauge is thus often used in mountainous terrain, where the savings in [civil engineering](/wiki/Civil_engineering "Civil engineering") work can be substantial. It is also used in sparsely populated areas, with low potential demand, and for temporary railways that will be removed after short-term use, such as for construction, the logging industry, the mining industry, or large-scale construction projects, especially in confined spaces (see [Temporary way – permanent way](#Temporary_way_–_permanent_way)).

For temporary railways which will be removed after short-term use, such as those used in logging, mining or large-scale construction projects, especially in confined spaces, such as when constructing the [Channel Tunnel](/wiki/Channel_Tunnel "Channel Tunnel"), a narrow-gauge railway is substantially cheaper and easier to install and remove. Such railways have almost vanished due to the capabilities of modern [trucks](/wiki/Truck "Truck"). In many countries, narrow-gauge railways were built as branch lines to feed traffic to standard-gauge lines due to lower construction costs. The choice was often not between a narrow- and standard-gauge railway, but between a narrow-gauge railway and none at all.

Broader gauge railways are generally more expensive to build, because they are usually heavier in construction, use larger [cars](/wiki/Railway_car "Railway car") and [locomotives](/wiki/Locomotive "Locomotive") (larger [loading gauge](/wiki/Loading_gauge "Loading gauge")), as well as larger [bridges](/wiki/Bridge "Bridge"), larger [tunnels](/wiki/Tunnel "Tunnel") (larger [structure gauge](/wiki/Structure_gauge "Structure gauge")). But broader gauges offer higher speed and capacity. For routes with high traffic, greater capacity may more than offset the higher initial cost of construction.

### Interchangeability

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Track_gauge&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Interchangeability")]

The [value](/wiki/Value_(economics) "Value (economics)") or [utility](/wiki/Utility "Utility") a user derives from a [good](/wiki/Goods "Goods") or [service](/wiki/Service_(economics) "Service (economics)") depends on the number of users of compatible products – the "[network effect](/wiki/Network_effect "Network effect")" in economics. Network effects are typically positive, resulting in a given user deriving more value from a product as other users join the same network.[[28]](#cite_note-30) At national levels, the network effect has resulted in commerce extending beyond regional and national boundaries. Increasingly, many governments and companies have made their railways' engineering and operational standards compatible in order to achieve interchangeability – hence faster, longer-distance train operation. A major barrier to achieving interchangeability, however, is [path dependence](/wiki/Path_dependence "Path dependence")[[29]](#cite_note-31) – in this context the persistence of an already adopted standard to which equipment, infrastructure and training has become aligned.

Since adopting a new standard is difficult and expensive, continuing with an existing standard can remain attractive, unless longer-term benefits are given appropriate weight. An example of the consequences of path dependence is the persistence in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") – the earliest nation to develop and adopt railway technologies – of [structure gauges](/wiki/Structure_gauge "Structure gauge") that are too small to allow the larger [rolling stock](/wiki/Rolling_stock "Rolling stock") of continental Europe to operate in the UK. The reduced cost, greater efficiency, and greater economic opportunity offered by the use of a common standard has resulted in the historical multitude of track gauges dwindling to a small number that predominate worldwide.

When interchangeability has not been achieved, freight and passengers must be transferred through time-consuming procedures requiring manual labour and substantial capital expenditure.[[30]](#cite_note-32) Some bulk commodities, such as [coal](/wiki/Coal "Coal"), [ore](/wiki/Ore "Ore"), and [gravel](/wiki/Gravel "Gravel"), can be mechanically [transshipped](/wiki/Transshipment "Transshipment"), but even this is time-consuming, and the equipment required for the transfer is often complex to maintain. If rail lines of different gauges coexist in a network and a [break of gauge](/wiki/Break_of_gauge "Break of gauge") exists, it is difficult in times of peak demand to move rolling stock to where it is needed.

Sufficient rolling stock must be available to meet a narrow-gauge railway's peak demand, which might be greater in comparison to a broader-gauge network, and the surplus equipment generates no cash flow during periods of low demand. In regions where narrow-gauge lines form a small part of the rail network (as was the case on Russia's [Sakhalin Railway](/wiki/Sakhalin_Railway "Sakhalin Railway")), extra cost is involved in designing, manufacturing or importing narrow-gauge equipment.

Solutions to interchangeability problems include [bogie exchanges](/wiki/Bogie_exchange "Bogie exchange"), a [rollbock](/wiki/Rollbock "Rollbock") system, [dual gauge](/wiki/Dual_gauge "Dual gauge"), [variable gauge](/wiki/Variable_gauge "Variable gauge"), or [gauge conversion](/wiki/Gauge_conversion "Gauge conversion").

## Future

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Track_gauge&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Future")]

Further convergence of rail gauge use seems likely, as countries seek to build inter-operable networks, and international organisations seek to build macro-regional and continental networks. Almost all new [high-speed rail](/wiki/High-speed_rail "High-speed rail") lines are built to standard gauge, except in Uzbekistan and Russia.

### Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Track_gauge&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Europe")]

The [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") has set out to develop inter-operable freight and passenger rail networks across its area, and is seeking to standardise gauge, signalling and electrical power systems. EU funds have been dedicated to assist [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania"), [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia "Latvia"), and [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia") in the building of some key railway lines ([Rail Baltica](/wiki/Rail_Baltica "Rail Baltica")) of [standard gauge](/wiki/Standard_gauge "Standard gauge"), and to assist Spain and Portugal in the construction of high-speed lines to connect Iberian cities to one another and to the French high-speed lines. The EU has developed plans for improved freight rail links between Spain, Portugal, and the rest of Europe.

### Trans-Asian Railway

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Track_gauge&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Trans-Asian Railway")]

Main article: [Trans-Asian Railway](/wiki/Trans-Asian_Railway "Trans-Asian Railway")

The United Nations [Economic and Social Commission for Asia and the Pacific](/wiki/Economic_and_Social_Commission_for_Asia_and_the_Pacific "Economic and Social Commission for Asia and the Pacific") (UNESCAP) is planning a [Trans-Asian Railway](/wiki/Trans-Asian_Railway "Trans-Asian Railway") that will link Europe and the Pacific, with a Northern Corridor from Europe to the Korean Peninsula, a Southern Corridor from Europe to Southeast Asia, and a North–South corridor from Northern Europe to the Persian Gulf. All these would encounter breaks of gauge as they cross Asia. Current plans have mechanized facilities at the breaks of gauge to move [containers](/wiki/Intermodal_container "Intermodal container") from train to train rather than widespread gauge conversion. The Northern Corridor through Russia already operates since before year 2000, with increasing volumes China–Europe.

### Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Track_gauge&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Africa")]

The [East African Railway Master Plan](/wiki/East_African_Railway_Master_Plan "East African Railway Master Plan") is a proposal for rebuilding and expanding railway lines connecting [Ethiopia](/wiki/Transport_in_Ethiopia "Transport in Ethiopia"), [Djibouti](/wiki/Transport_in_Djibouti "Transport in Djibouti"), [Kenya](/wiki/Transport_in_Kenya "Transport in Kenya"), [Uganda](/wiki/Transport_in_Uganda "Transport in Uganda"), [Rwanda](/wiki/Transport_in_Rwanda "Transport in Rwanda"), [Burundi](/wiki/Transport_in_Burundi "Transport in Burundi"), [Tanzania](/wiki/Transport_in_Tanzania "Transport in Tanzania"), [South Sudan](/wiki/Transport_in_South_Sudan "Transport in South Sudan") and beyond.[[37]](#cite_note-39) The plan is managed by infrastructure ministers from participating [East African Community](/wiki/East_African_Community "East African Community") countries in association with transport consultation firm [CPCS Transcom](/wiki/CPCS_Transcom "CPCS Transcom").[[38]](#cite_note-40) Older railways are of 1,000 mm (3 ft 3+3⁄8 in) [metre gauge](/wiki/Metre_gauge "Metre gauge") or [3 ft 6 in](/wiki/3_ft_6_in_gauge_railways "3 ft 6 in gauge railways") (1,067 mm) gauge. Newly rebuilt lines will use [standard gauge](/wiki/Standard_gauge "Standard gauge"). Regular freight and passenger services began on the standard gauge [Mombasa–Nairobi](/wiki/Mombasa-Nairobi_Standard_Gauge_Railway "Mombasa-Nairobi Standard Gauge Railway") railway in 2017 and on the standard gauge [Addis Ababa–Djibouti](/wiki/Addis_Ababa-Djibouti_Railway "Addis Ababa-Djibouti Railway") railway in 2018.

Lines for iron ore to [Kribi](/wiki/Kribi "Kribi") in [Cameroon](/wiki/Transport_in_Cameroon "Transport in Cameroon") are likely to be 1,435 mm (4 ft 8+1⁄2 in) [standard gauge](/wiki/Standard_gauge "Standard gauge") with a likely connection to the same port from the 1,000 mm (3 ft 3+3⁄8 in) [metre gauge](/wiki/Metre_gauge "Metre gauge") Cameroon system.[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

[Nigeria's railways](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Nigeria "Rail transport in Nigeria") are mostly [3 ft 6 in](/wiki/3_ft_6_in_gauge_railways "3 ft 6 in gauge railways") (1,067 mm) Cape gauge. The [Lagos–Kano Standard Gauge Railway](/wiki/Lagos%E2%80%93Kano_Standard_Gauge_Railway "Lagos–Kano Standard Gauge Railway") is a [gauge conversion](/wiki/Track_gauge_conversion "Track gauge conversion") project by the Nigerian Government to create a north–south standard gauge rail link. The first converted segment, between [Abuja](/wiki/Abuja "Abuja") and [Kaduna](/wiki/Kaduna_(city) "Kaduna (city)"), was completed in July 2016.

The [African Union](/wiki/African_Union "African Union") has a 50-year plan to connect the capital cities and major centres by high-speed railways.
